expanded_description: Angle pairs have special relationships defined by their measures and positions. Complementary angles sum to 90°, supplementary angles sum to 180°, and vertical angles are opposite each other when two lines intersect—they are always congruent. Understanding these relationships is essential for solving equations, proving theorems, and analyzing geometric figures.

core_concept.principle: Angle pairs are classified by how their measures relate (complementary: sum to 90°; supplementary: sum to 180°) or by their geometric position (vertical: opposite angles formed by intersecting lines; adjacent: share a vertex and side; linear pair: adjacent and supplementary). These relationships provide powerful tools for finding unknown angle measures.
core_concept.explanation: Think of complementary angles as two puzzle pieces that form a right-angle corner. Supplementary angles form a straight line when placed adjacent—like opening a book flat on a table. Vertical angles are the matching angles where two lines cross, like an X—the top and bottom angles are equal, and the left and right angles are equal. Recognizing these patterns turns complex diagrams into solvable equations.

image.alt: Four panels: (1) Two angles forming a right angle — Complementary, (2) Two angles forming a straight line — Supplementary, (3) Two intersecting lines with opposite angles marked equal — Vertical angles, (4) Two angles sharing a vertex and side — Adjacent. Each labeled with definition.
image.caption: Angle Pair Relationships: Complementary (sum 90°), Supplementary (sum 180°), Vertical (congruent), and Adjacent (share vertex and side).

sections.introduction: Before studying angle pairs with special measure relationships, we must understand how angles can be positioned relative to each other. Adjacent angles share a vertex and a side. When adjacent angles form a straight line, they are called a linear pair and are always supplementary.

introduction_image.alt: Two diagrams side by side: (1) Two angles sharing vertex B and side BC — labeled 'Adjacent Angles: ∠ABC and ∠CBD share vertex B and side BC→'. (2) Two adjacent angles forming a straight line — labeled 'Linear Pair: adjacent angles that form a straight line, always supplementary (sum 180°)'.
introduction_image.caption: Adjacent angles share a vertex and a common side. A linear pair is a special case of adjacent angles that form a straight line.

key_concept.definition: Adjacent angles are two angles in the same plane that share a common vertex and a common side but have no interior points in common. A linear pair is a pair of adjacent angles whose non-common sides are opposite rays, forming a straight line. By the Linear Pair Postulate, angles in a linear pair are always supplementary (sum to 180°).
key_concept.context: The concept of adjacency is essential for correctly identifying angle relationships in complex diagrams. The Linear Pair Postulate is one of the most frequently used tools in geometry—whenever you see a straight line divided by a ray, the two adjacent angles formed sum to 180°.

image.alt: Left: Two adjacent angles ∠ABC and ∠CBD sharing vertex B and side BC. Their interiors do not overlap. Right: A linear pair — same as adjacent but the non-common sides BA→ and BD→ are opposite rays forming line AD. Equation: m∠ABC + m∠CBD = 180°.
image.caption: Adjacent angles share a vertex and side. Linear pairs are adjacent and supplementary.

classifications.value: Two angles that share a common vertex and a common side, with no overlapping interiors.
characteristics: Share exactly one vertex and one side (a ray)
Lie on opposite sides of the common side
Have no interior points in common — they do not overlap
Adjacent is about position, not measure
classifications.behavior: Adjacency describes how two angles are positioned relative to each other. Angles can be adjacent without having any special sum relationship. However, many special angle pairs (linear pairs, adjacent complementary or supplementary angles) are also adjacent.

examples.process: Identifying adjacent angles in a diagram
examples.explanation:
∠ABC and ∠CBD are adjacent because they share vertex B and common side BC→.

∠ABC and ∠ABD are NOT adjacent because they overlap (∠ABC is inside ∠ABD).

Adjacency requires the angles to be side by side, not one inside the other.

image.alt: Diagram with point B and three rays: BA→, BC→, BD→ in clockwise order. ∠ABC and ∠CBD are labeled 'Adjacent — share BC→'. ∠ABC and ∠ABD are labeled 'NOT adjacent — overlap (∠ABC is contained in ∠ABD)'.
image.caption: Adjacent angles share a side and do not overlap. Nested angles are not adjacent.

classifications.value: A pair of adjacent angles whose non-common sides form opposite rays, creating a straight line.
characteristics: Always adjacent (share vertex and a common side)
The non-common sides are opposite rays forming a line
By the Linear Pair Postulate, they are always supplementary: sum to 180°
One of the most common angle relationships in geometry
classifications.behavior: Whenever a ray extends from a point on a line, it creates a linear pair. The two angles formed are supplementary. If one angle is known, subtract from 180° to find the other. This is the foundation for solving many angle problems.

examples.explanation:
Points A, B, C are collinear with B between A and C. Ray BD extends from B upward.

∠ABD and ∠DBC form a linear pair.

m∠ABD + m∠DBC = 180° (always).

If m∠ABD = 120°, then m∠DBC = 180° − 120° = 60°.

image.alt: Horizontal line AC with B in the middle. Ray BD extends upward. ∠ABD (obtuse, 120°) and ∠DBC (acute, 60°) are marked. Equation: 120° + 60° = 180°. Label: 'Linear Pair — always supplementary.'
image.caption: Linear pair: adjacent angles on a straight line always sum to 180°.

image.alt: Comprehensive diagram: Three rays from point B. Two adjacent angles marked (∠ABC and ∠CBD). The non-common sides highlighted to show they form line AD — confirming linear pair. Notation: shared side, vertex, opposite rays labeled.
image.caption: Adjacent angles share a side. When non-common sides are opposite rays, the pair is a linear pair — always 180°.

sections.introduction: Complementary angles are two angles whose measures sum to exactly 90°. They can be adjacent, forming a right angle together, or they can be separate. The relationship is about measure, not position—any two angles summing to 90° are complementary.

introduction_image.alt: Two panels: (1) Adjacent complementary angles — 30° and 60° sharing a side, forming a right angle (square mark). (2) Non-adjacent complementary angles — two separate angles 30° and 60° in different locations. Both labeled 'Complementary: 30° + 60° = 90°'.
introduction_image.caption: Complementary angles sum to 90°. They can be adjacent (forming a right angle) or separate.

key_concept.definition: Complementary angles are two angles whose measures sum to exactly 90°. Each angle is the complement of the other. If m∠A + m∠B = 90°, then ∠A and ∠B are complementary.
key_concept.context: The complement relationship is used to find unknown angles in right triangles (the two acute angles are complementary), in perpendicular line problems, and in algebraic equations. The phrase 'complement of x' means 90° − x.

image.alt: Right angle formed by two adjacent complementary angles labeled x and y. Equation: x + y = 90°. Below: the complement of x is 90° − x. Examples: complement of 35° is 55°, complement of 70° is 20°.
image.caption: Complementary angles sum to 90°. The complement of an angle is 90° minus that angle.

classifications.value: To find the complement of an angle, subtract its measure from 90°.
characteristics: Complement of x° = (90 − x)°
Both angles in a complementary pair are acute (unless one is 0° or 90°)
In a right triangle, the two non-right angles are always complementary
If two angles are complementary to the same angle, they are congruent
classifications.behavior: Given one angle, its complement is 90° minus that angle. Two complementary angles can be represented algebraically as x and 90 − x. This is useful for setting up equations.

examples.explanation:
Complement of 25° = 90° − 25° = 65°.

Complement of 48° = 90° − 48° = 42°.

Complement of x° = (90 − x)°.

image.alt: Three examples: (1) Angle 25° with complement 65° shown, (2) Angle 48° with complement 42° shown, (3) General formula: complement of x = 90° − x.
image.caption: The complement is found by subtracting from 90°.

classifications.value: Set up equations using the fact that the two angle measures sum to 90°.
characteristics: Let one angle be x, the other be 90 − x (or use two variables with x + y = 90)
If one angle is described in terms of the other, write an equation and solve
Always check that both resulting angles are positive and less than 90°
classifications.behavior: Complementary angle problems translate directly to equations: x + y = 90. If one angle is '12 more than the other,' write x + (x + 12) = 90. Solve for x, then find both angles.

examples.explanation:
'One angle is three times its complement. Find both angles.'

Let the smaller angle = x. Its complement = 3x.

x + 3x = 90° → 4x = 90° → x = 22.5°

Angles: 22.5° and 3(22.5) = 67.5°. Check: 22.5° + 67.5° = 90° ✓

image.alt: Two adjacent angles labeled x and 3x forming a right angle. Equation: x + 3x = 90, solving to x = 22.5°, angles 22.5° and 67.5°. Both shown in diagram with correct proportions.
image.caption: Algebraic complementary problem: one angle is three times its complement.

worked_examples.title: Complementary Angle Problems
worked_examples.problem:
1) Find the complement of 37°.

2) Two angles are complementary. One angle is 14° more than the other. Find both angles.

3) ∠A and ∠B are complementary. m∠A = 2x + 8, m∠B = 3x − 3. Find x and each angle measure.

step_by_step.step: 2) Set up equation for word problem
step_by_step.explanation:
Let the smaller angle = x. The larger = x + 14°.

x + (x + 14) = 90

2x + 14 = 90 → 2x = 76 → x = 38°

step_by_step.step: 3) Set up equation with algebraic expressions

image.alt: Three diagrams: (1) 37° angle with complement 53°. (2) Complementary angles 38° and 52° forming right angle. (3) Angles with algebraic expressions: m∠A = 42°, m∠B = 48° forming right angle.
image.caption: Worked examples: Finding complements, word problems, and algebraic complementary equations.

worked_examples.concept_applied: Complementary angles sum to 90°. Set up equation: angle + complement = 90°. Use x and 90−x, or set two expressions equal to 90.

sections.introduction: Supplementary angles are two angles whose measures sum to exactly 180°. A linear pair is the most common example—two adjacent angles forming a straight line. But supplementary angles can also be separate; only the sum matters.

introduction_image.alt: Two panels: (1) Linear pair — adjacent supplementary angles 70° and 110° forming a straight line. (2) Non-adjacent supplementary angles — two separate angles 70° and 110° in different locations. Both labeled 'Supplementary: 70° + 110° = 180°'.
introduction_image.caption: Supplementary angles sum to 180°. Linear pairs are always supplementary. Non-adjacent angles can also be supplementary.

key_concept.definition: Supplementary angles are two angles whose measures sum to exactly 180°. Each angle is the supplement of the other. If m∠A + m∠B = 180°, then ∠A and ∠B are supplementary. A linear pair is a special case of adjacent supplementary angles.
key_concept.context: The supplement relationship is used extensively with parallel lines (consecutive interior angles are supplementary), in triangle geometry (exterior angle and adjacent interior angle are supplementary), and in solving for unknown angles along a straight line.

image.alt: Straight line formed by adjacent supplementary angles labeled x and y. Equation: x + y = 180°. Below: the supplement of x is 180° − x. Examples: supplement of 45° is 135°, supplement of 110° is 70°.
image.caption: Supplementary angles sum to 180°. The supplement of an angle is 180° minus that angle.

classifications.value: To find the supplement of an angle, subtract its measure from 180°.
characteristics: Supplement of x° = (180 − x)°
If one angle is acute, its supplement is obtuse (and vice versa)
If an angle is 90°, it is its own supplement (90° + 90° = 180°)
If two angles are supplementary to the same angle, they are congruent
classifications.behavior: Given one angle, its supplement is 180° minus that angle. Two supplementary angles can be represented algebraically as x and 180 − x. Every linear pair is supplementary, but supplementary angles need not be adjacent.

examples.explanation:
Supplement of 45° = 180° − 45° = 135°.

Supplement of 112° = 180° − 112° = 68°.

Supplement of x° = (180 − x)°.

image.alt: Three examples: (1) Angle 45° with supplement 135° shown, (2) Angle 112° with supplement 68° shown, (3) General formula: supplement of x = 180° − x.
image.caption: The supplement is found by subtracting from 180°.

classifications.value: Set up equations using the fact that the two angle measures sum to 180°.
characteristics: Let one angle be x, the other be 180 − x (or use two variables with x + y = 180)
If one angle is described in terms of the other, write an equation and solve
Always check that both resulting angles are positive and less than 180°
classifications.behavior: Supplementary angle problems translate to equations: x + y = 180. Common patterns: an angle is 'twice its supplement' or angles are in a given ratio. Set up and solve algebraically.

examples.explanation:
'An angle is 30° less than twice its supplement. Find the angle.'

Let the angle = x. Its supplement = 180 − x.

x = 2(180 − x) − 30

x = 360 − 2x − 30 → x = 330 − 2x → 3x = 330 → x = 110°

Supplement = 180° − 110° = 70°. Check: 2(70) − 30 = 140 − 30 = 110° ✓

image.alt: Two adjacent angles forming a straight line. Angle labeled x = 110°, supplement labeled 70°. Equation and work shown: x = 2(180−x) − 30, solving to x = 110°.
image.caption: Algebraic supplementary problem: angle is 30° less than twice its supplement.

worked_examples.title: Supplementary Angle Problems
worked_examples.problem:
1) Find the supplement of 73°.

2) Two supplementary angles are in a ratio of 5:4. Find both angles.

3) ∠P and ∠Q are supplementary. m∠P = 4x + 10, m∠Q = 2x + 20. Find x and each angle measure.

step_by_step.step: 3) Set up equation with algebraic expressions

image.alt: Three diagrams: (1) 73° angle with supplement 107°. (2) Supplementary angles 100° and 80° forming a straight line (ratio 5:4). (3) Angles with algebraic expressions: m∠P = 110°, m∠Q = 70° forming a linear pair.
image.caption: Worked examples: Finding supplements, ratio problems, and algebraic supplementary equations.

worked_examples.concept_applied: Supplementary angles sum to 180°. Set up equation: angle + supplement = 180°. Use x and 180−x, ratio methods, or set two algebraic expressions equal to 180.

sections.introduction: When two lines intersect, they form two pairs of opposite angles called vertical angles. Vertical angles are always congruent—they have equal measure. This powerful relationship allows you to find unknown angle measures at any intersection.

introduction_image.alt: Two intersecting lines forming an X. Four angles labeled: top and bottom marked with matching arcs and equal labels, left and right marked with different matching arcs and equal labels. Equation: 'Vertical angles are congruent: ∠1 ≅ ∠3, ∠2 ≅ ∠4'.
introduction_image.caption: When two lines intersect, opposite (vertical) angles are always congruent.

key_concept.definition: Vertical angles are two non-adjacent angles formed by two intersecting lines. They are opposite each other at the vertex. The Vertical Angles Theorem states that vertical angles are always congruent: m∠1 = m∠3 and m∠2 = m∠4.
key_concept.context: The Vertical Angles Theorem is one of the most commonly used results in geometry. It appears in proofs about parallel lines, triangles, quadrilaterals, and circles. Whenever lines cross, the opposite angles are equal—a fact that simplifies countless problems.

image.alt: Two intersecting lines with four angles numbered 1 through 4 clockwise. ∠1 and ∠3 are vertical (opposite), highlighted in blue. ∠2 and ∠4 are vertical, highlighted in red. Equations: m∠1 = m∠3, m∠2 = m∠4. Also: adjacent pairs are supplementary.
image.caption: Vertical angles are opposite each other at an intersection — always congruent.

classifications.value: Vertical angles are the non-adjacent pairs at the intersection of two lines. They are 'across from' each other.
characteristics: Formed by two intersecting lines
Two pairs of vertical angles at each intersection
Vertical angles share a vertex but NOT a side
They are opposite each other — if angles are numbered 1-4 clockwise, (1,3) and (2,4) are vertical
classifications.behavior: To identify vertical angles, look at the X formed by intersecting lines. The angles that are directly across from each other (not next to each other) are vertical angles. Adjacent angles at the intersection form linear pairs and are supplementary.

examples.process: Picking out vertical angles at an intersection
examples.explanation:
At the intersection of two lines, there are four angles.

∠1 and ∠3 are vertical (they are opposite each other).

∠2 and ∠4 are vertical (they are opposite each other).

∠1 and ∠2 are adjacent (linear pair), not vertical.

image.alt: Two intersecting lines with angles numbered 1 (top), 2 (right), 3 (bottom), 4 (left). ∠1 and ∠3 highlighted as 'Vertical Pair 1'. ∠2 and ∠4 highlighted as 'Vertical Pair 2'. Adjacent pairs shown as 'Linear Pairs (not vertical)'.
image.caption: Vertical angles are opposite. Adjacent angles at the intersection are linear pairs.

classifications.value: If two angles are vertical, they are congruent. Set their measures equal to solve for unknowns.
characteristics: m∠1 = m∠3 and m∠2 = m∠4 (the vertical pairs are equal)
Also: adjacent angles are supplementary: m∠1 + m∠2 = 180°, etc.
Knowing one angle at an intersection gives all four: subtract from 180° for adjacent, vertical is equal
classifications.behavior: The Vertical Angles Theorem transforms diagrams into equations. If one angle is known, its vertical partner is the same. Its adjacent partners are supplementary (180° minus the known angle). This solves all four angles instantly.

examples.process: Solving all angles at an intersection
examples.explanation:
Two lines intersect. One angle measures 65°.

Its vertical angle = 65° (congruent).

Each adjacent angle = 180° − 65° = 115° (linear pair).

The four angles are: 65°, 115°, 65°, 115°.

image.alt: Two intersecting lines. Top angle = 65°. Vertical (bottom) = 65° (equal). Left and right angles = 115° each (180° − 65°). All four labeled. Equation notes: 'Vertical → equal', 'Linear pair → supplementary.'
image.caption: Given one angle at an intersection, vertical angles give one pair, supplements give the other pair.

classifications.value: When vertical angle measures are given as expressions, set them equal to each other and solve.
characteristics: Set the two vertical angle expressions equal: expression1 = expression2
Solve for the variable
Use the value to find all four angle measures at the intersection
Can also set adjacent angles (linear pair) equal to 180°
classifications.behavior: Vertical angle algebra problems use equality: the expressions for opposite angles are set equal. This is different from complementary (sum = 90°) or supplementary (sum = 180°) problems. Recognition of vertical angles triggers an equation of equality.

examples.explanation:
Two lines intersect. m∠1 = 3x + 10, m∠3 = 5x − 20. ∠1 and ∠3 are vertical.

Since vertical angles are congruent: 3x + 10 = 5x − 20

10 + 20 = 5x − 3x → 30 = 2x → x = 15

m∠1 = 3(15) + 10 = 55°, m∠3 = 5(15) − 20 = 55°

Adjacent angles (∠2 and ∠4) = 180° − 55° = 125° each.

image.alt: Two intersecting lines. ∠1 = 3x + 10 (top), ∠3 = 5x − 20 (bottom) — vertical pair. Equation: 3x + 10 = 5x − 20, solving x = 15. All four angles labeled: 55°, 125°, 55°, 125°.
image.caption: Algebraic vertical angles: set opposite angles equal and solve for x.

worked_examples.title: Vertical Angle Problems
worked_examples.problem:
1) Two lines intersect. One angle measures 78°. Find the other three angles.

2) Two lines intersect. m∠1 = 4x + 5, m∠3 = 2x + 45. ∠1 and ∠3 are vertical. Find x and all four angle measures.

3) At an intersection, m∠A = 3x + 15, and m∠B (adjacent to ∠A) = 2x. Find x and all four angles.

step_by_step.step: 1) Solve for all angles at intersection
step_by_step.explanation:
Given angle = 78°. Its vertical angle = 78° (congruent).

Each adjacent angle = 180° − 78° = 102° (linear pair).

Four angles: 78°, 102°, 78°, 102°.

step_by_step.step: 3) Adjacent angles are a linear pair
step_by_step.explanation:
∠A and ∠B are adjacent at an intersection, forming a linear pair.

(3x + 15) + 2x = 180 → 5x + 15 = 180 → 5x = 165 → x = 33

image.alt: Three diagrams: (1) Intersection with one angle 78°, all four solved: 78°, 102°, 78°, 102°. (2) Algebraic vertical angles solving to x = 20, angles 85° and 95°. (3) Linear pair with algebra solving to x = 33, angles 114° and 66°.
image.caption: Worked examples: Finding all angles at an intersection, vertical angle algebra, and linear pair algebra.

worked_examples.concept_applied: Vertical angles are congruent (equal). Adjacent angles at an intersection are supplementary (sum to 180°). Set vertical expressions equal; set adjacent expressions to sum to 180.

sections.introduction: In real geometric diagrams, multiple angle pair relationships often appear together. Solving complex problems requires identifying which relationships apply—vertical, linear pair, complementary, supplementary—and using them in combination to find all unknown angles.

introduction_image.alt: A diagram showing two intersecting lines with a ray from the intersection, creating multiple angles. Labels identify: vertical pairs, linear pairs, complementary pair (if marked), and adjacent angles. Multiple relationships coexist in one diagram.
introduction_image.caption: In complex diagrams, multiple angle relationships work together. Identify each pair and apply the correct rule.

key_concept.definition: Angle pair relationships—vertical, linear pair, complementary, and supplementary—are not mutually exclusive. A single angle can be part of multiple relationships. The key to solving complex diagrams is identifying each relationship and using the appropriate equation for each.
key_concept.context: In geometry proofs and problems, you rarely work with isolated angle pairs. Lines intersect, rays bisect, and perpendicular lines create right angles. Systematic identification of all relationships is the first step to solving for unknown angles.

image.alt: A diagram with intersecting lines and a perpendicular ray. Several angle pairs are highlighted: one vertical pair, two linear pairs, complementary pairs forming 90°. A checklist overlay: '1) Find vertical pairs → set equal. 2) Find linear pairs → sum to 180°. 3) Find complementary → sum to 90°. 4) Solve system.'
image.caption: Solving complex diagrams: Identify all relationships, then set up and solve the resulting equations.

classifications.value: A step-by-step strategy for untangling diagrams with multiple angle relationships.
characteristics: Step 1: Identify all vertical angle pairs — mark with matching arcs and set equal
Step 2: Identify all linear pairs — mark with supplementary symbol and set sums to 180°
Step 3: Identify complementary pairs if perpendicular lines are present
Step 4: Use known angles to find unknowns by substituting into the relationships
Step 5: Verify all results — check that each relationship holds
classifications.behavior: Work outward from known angles. Use vertical angles to duplicate known measures. Use linear pairs to find supplementary measures. Use complementary pairs near right angles. Check consistency across all relationships.

examples.explanation:
Two lines intersect. A ray from the vertex bisects one of the four angles. Given one angle = 40°.

Step 1: Vertical to 40° = 40°.

Step 2: Linear pairs with 40° = 140° each (two of them).

Step 3: The bisector splits the 40° angle into 20° and 20°.

Step 4: Linear pairs with the 20° angles = 160° each.

image.alt: Two intersecting lines with a ray bisecting the top angle. Given 40° at top. Solution steps shown: vertical = 40°, linear pairs = 140°, bisector creates 20° and 20°, their supplements = 160°. All angles labeled.
image.caption: Complex example: intersection with angle bisector. Use vertical, linear pair, and bisector relationships.

classifications.value: When multiple angle measures involve variables, set up a system of equations from the different relationships.
characteristics: Use vertical relationships to set expressions equal
Use linear pair or complementary relationships to set sums to 180° or 90°
Solve the resulting system of equations
Substitute to find all unknown measures
classifications.behavior: A single diagram can produce multiple equations. For example, vertical angles give equality equations, while linear pairs give supplementary equations. Solve the system algebraically.

examples.explanation:
Two lines intersect. m∠1 = 2x, m∠2 = x + y, m∠3 = 3y, and m∠4 = x − y.

Vertical: ∠1 = ∠3 → 2x = 3y

Linear pair: ∠1 + ∠2 = 180° → 2x + (x + y) = 180° → 3x + y = 180°

Solve: From 2x = 3y, y = 2x/3. Substitute: 3x + 2x/3 = 180° → (9x + 2x)/3 = 180° → 11x/3 = 180° → x ≈ 49.1°, y ≈ 32.7°

image.alt: Two intersecting lines with four angles expressed algebraically: ∠1 = 2x, ∠2 = x + y, ∠3 = 3y, ∠4 = x − y. Equations written: 2x = 3y (vertical) and 2x + x + y = 180° (linear pair). Solution steps shown.
image.caption: Multiple relationships create a system of equations. Solve using substitution.

worked_examples.title: Solving Complex Angle Pair Problems
worked_examples.problem:
1) Two lines intersect. A ray bisects one of the four angles. The bisected angle measures 80°. Find all angle measures in the diagram.

2) Two lines intersect to form four angles. m∠1 = 2x + 10, m∠2 = 3x − 20. Find x and all four angles.

3) At an intersection, m∠A = 5x − 15, and m∠B (vertical to ∠A) = 2x + 30. Find x and all four angles.

step_by_step.explanation:
The bisected angle = 80°, so each half = 40°.

The vertical angle opposite the 80° angle = 80°.

The linear pair with 80°: 180° − 80° = 100° (two angles).

The bisector creates two 40° angles; each has a linear pair of 140°.

All angles: 40°, 40° (the bisected halves), 100° (adjacent), 80° (vertical to whole), 140° (linear with halves).

∠1 and ∠2 are adjacent at the intersection, forming a linear pair.

(2x + 10) + (3x − 20) = 180

5x − 10 = 180 → 5x = 190 → x = 38

image.alt: Three diagrams: (1) Intersection with bisected 80° angle, all measures shown. (2) Linear pair with algebra: x = 38, angles 86°, 94°, 86°, 94°. (3) Vertical pair with algebra: x = 15, angles 60°, 120°, 60°, 120°.
image.caption: Complex angle pair problems: combining bisectors, linear pairs, and vertical angles with algebra.

worked_examples.final_answer:
1) Angles: 40°, 40°, 100°, 80°, 140°, 140° (six angles in diagram with bisector)

2) x = 38, angles: 86°, 94°, 86°, 94°

3) x = 15, angles: 60°, 120°, 60°, 120°
worked_examples.concept_applied: Multiple angle pair relationships in one diagram: vertical angles (set equal), linear pairs (sum to 180°), and angle bisectors (split into equal halves).

sections.introduction: Angle pair problems are a frequent source of errors. Mixing up the relationships, misidentifying pairs, and applying the wrong equation are common pitfalls. Learning to recognize these patterns helps avoid unnecessary mistakes.

key_concept.definition: The most common angle pair errors involve: confusing complementary and supplementary sums, misidentifying vertical vs. adjacent angles, applying the wrong equation type, and forgetting that vertical angles are always congruent regardless of whether the diagram 'looks' symmetric.
key_concept.context: These errors often stem from rushing to set up equations without carefully identifying which relationship applies. A systematic approach—identify the relationship first, then write the equation—prevents most mistakes.

image.alt: Four warning panels: (1) Complementary (90°) ≠ Supplementary (180°), (2) Vertical (opposite, equal) ≠ Adjacent (next to, sum to 180°), (3) Wrong equation: 'Set equal' for vertical, 'Sum to 180°' for linear pair, (4) Diagram not to scale — vertical angles are equal even if drawn differently.
image.caption: Common angle pair mistakes: wrong sum, misidentified pairs, wrong equation type, and trusting inaccurate sketches.

classifications.value: Using 180° when the relationship is complementary (90°), or vice versa.
characteristics: Error: Writing x + y = 180° for two angles that form a right angle
Error: Writing x + y = 90° for two angles on a straight line
Correct: Look at the geometric context — right angle corner = 90°, straight line = 180°
classifications.behavior: The sum depends on what the angles form together. If they form a right angle (square mark present), sum = 90°. If they form a straight line (linear pair), sum = 180°. Don't memorize—visualize.

examples.process: Identifying the correct sum from the diagram
examples.explanation:
Angles forming a right angle (marked with a square) → complementary, sum = 90°.

Angles forming a straight line → supplementary, sum = 180°.

Angles that are NOT adjacent → check if they are vertical (equal) or if sum is specified in the problem.

image.alt: Two diagrams side by side: (1) Two angles forming a right angle with square mark — labeled 'Right angle = 90°, use complementary'. (2) Two angles forming a straight line — labeled 'Straight line = 180°, use supplementary'. Red warning: 'Check the geometric context.'
image.caption: Right angle (square mark) = 90°. Straight line = 180°. The diagram tells you which sum.

classifications.note: Solution: Check the diagram. Square corner mark → 90° (complementary). Straight line → 180° (supplementary). No geometric context given → use the word 'complementary' or 'supplementary' in the problem statement.

classifications.value: Setting vertical angles to sum to 180° instead of equal, or setting adjacent angles equal instead of summing to 180°.
characteristics: Error: Writing m∠1 + m∠3 = 180° when ∠1 and ∠3 are vertical (should be equal)
Error: Writing m∠1 = m∠2 when ∠1 and ∠2 are adjacent (should sum to 180° if linear pair)
Correct: Vertical → equal. Adjacent at intersection → linear pair → supplementary.
classifications.behavior: Vertical angles are opposite each other. Adjacent angles share a side. At an intersection of two lines, opposite = equal, adjacent = supplementary. Mixing these up leads to completely wrong answers.

examples.explanation:
∠1 and ∠3 are opposite (vertical) → m∠1 = m∠3.

∠1 and ∠2 are adjacent → m∠1 + m∠2 = 180° (linear pair).

∠2 and ∠4 are opposite (vertical) → m∠2 = m∠4.

∠3 and ∠4 are adjacent → m∠3 + m∠4 = 180° (linear pair).

image.alt: Two intersecting lines with angles numbered 1-4. ∠1 and ∠3 connected with 'Equal (vertical)'. ∠1 and ∠2 connected with 'Sum to 180° (linear pair)'. Red X over incorrect equations: ∠1 + ∠3 = 180° and ∠1 = ∠2.
image.caption: Vertical = equal. Adjacent (linear pair) = sum to 180°. Never reverse these equations.

classifications.note: Solution: At an intersection: across from each other = vertical → EQUAL. Next to each other = linear pair → SUM TO 180°. Draw arrows across the intersection to see which angles face each other.

classifications.type: Forgetting Vertical Angles Are Always Congruent
classifications.value: Thinking vertical angles might not be equal if the diagram looks asymmetrical or is not drawn to scale.
characteristics: Error: 'The diagram doesn't look like those angles are equal, so maybe they aren't.'
Error: Measuring angles from a diagram instead of using the theorem
Correct: The Vertical Angles Theorem is a proven fact — vertical angles are ALWAYS congruent, regardless of how the diagram is drawn.
classifications.behavior: Diagrams are often not drawn to scale. The Vertical Angles Theorem guarantees equality regardless of appearance. Never use a protractor on a diagram unless told it is to scale—use the theorem.

examples.explanation:
A diagram shows two intersecting lines. The top and bottom angles look different sizes.

Despite appearances, by the Vertical Angles Theorem, they are congruent.

If the problem says they are vertical, set their measures equal—don't measure the diagram.

image.alt: Two intersecting lines drawn with clearly different-looking opposite angles (one narrow, one wide — a poorly drawn sketch). A red X over using a protractor. Caption: 'Diagrams may not be to scale. Vertical angles are ALWAYS congruent by theorem.'
image.caption: Never rely on diagram appearance. Vertical angles are congruent by theorem, always.

classifications.note: Solution: Trust the theorem, not the sketch. If two angles are vertical, they are equal—period. If a problem states angles are vertical, immediately write: expression1 = expression2.

classifications.value: Using equality (set equal) for supplementary relationships, or sum-to-180 for vertical relationships.
characteristics: Error: For complementary angles, writing angle1 = angle2 (should be sum = 90°)
Error: For vertical angles, writing angle1 + angle2 = 180° (should be equal)
Correct: Identify the relationship type, then use the corresponding equation form
classifications.behavior: Each angle pair relationship has a specific equation type: Complementary → sum = 90°. Supplementary / Linear Pair → sum = 180°. Vertical → set equal. Angle Bisector → set equal. Matching the equation to the relationship is essential.

examples.explanation:
Complementary: m∠A + m∠B = 90°

Supplementary / Linear Pair: m∠A + m∠B = 180°

Vertical Angles: m∠A = m∠B

Angle Bisector: m∠A = m∠B

Match the relationship to the equation. Don't guess.

image.alt: A decision flowchart: 'What is the relationship?' → Vertical or Bisector → 'Set equal: expr1 = expr2'. → Complementary → 'Sum to 90°: expr1 + expr2 = 90'. → Supplementary or Linear Pair → 'Sum to 180°: expr1 + expr2 = 180'. Each path shown with example.
image.caption: Use a decision process: identify the relationship, then choose the correct equation form.

classifications.note: Solution: Before writing any equation, state the relationship: 'These are vertical angles, so they are equal.' Then write the equality equation. The words guide the math.

worked_examples.title: Correcting Angle Pair Errors
worked_examples.problem:
A student makes these errors. Identify and correct each one.

1) Two angles form a right angle. Student writes: m∠A + m∠B = 180°.

2) ∠1 and ∠3 are vertical angles. Student writes: m∠1 + m∠3 = 180°.

3) Given ∠C and ∠D are complementary, student writes: m∠C = m∠D.

4) Student measures vertical angles with a protractor and says they 'look different, so the theorem must not apply here.'

step_by_step.explanation:
Error: A right angle measures 90°, not 180°. The student used the supplementary sum.

Correct: Angles forming a right angle are complementary. m∠A + m∠B = 90°.

step_by_step.step: 2) Vertical angles are equal, not supplementary
step_by_step.explanation:
Error: Vertical angles are congruent (equal), not supplementary.

Correct: By the Vertical Angles Theorem, m∠1 = m∠3. Adjacent angles at the intersection (like ∠1 and ∠2) are supplementary and sum to 180°.

Error: Complementary describes a sum relationship (90°), not an equality relationship.

Correct: If ∠C and ∠D are complementary, then m∠C + m∠D = 90°. Equality would apply if they were vertical angles or an angle bisector.

step_by_step.step: 4) Trust the theorem, not the diagram
step_by_step.explanation:
Error: The Vertical Angles Theorem is a mathematical truth—it applies regardless of how a diagram is drawn. Diagrams in geometry problems are often not to scale.

Correct: If two angles are vertical, they are congruent by theorem. Never rely on visual measurement of a diagram.

image.alt: Four correction panels: (1) Right angle = 90° (complementary), (2) Vertical angles marked equal, (3) Complementary angles summing to 90°, (4) Note: 'Trust the theorem, not the sketch'. All corrections shown with green checks.
image.caption: Corrected angle pair errors: right sums, vertical equality, complementary sum, and theorem over appearance.

worked_examples.final_answer:
1) m∠A + m∠B = 90° (complementary, right angle)

2) m∠1 = m∠3 (vertical angles are congruent)

3) m∠C + m∠D = 90° (complementary = sum to 90°)

4) Vertical angles are always congruent by theorem, regardless of how the diagram looks.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Right angle → 90° sum. Vertical → equality. Complementary → 90° sum. Theorems override diagram appearance. Match equation type to relationship.

sections.introduction: Angle pair relationships appear constantly in architecture, engineering, art, and everyday problem-solving. Recognizing complementary, supplementary, and vertical angles in real situations allows for measurement without direct access and design with precision.

categories.description: Buildings, bridges, and structures rely on angle relationships for stability, aesthetics, and functionality. Right angles (complementary pairs) and supplementary angles are designed into load-bearing elements.

examples.explanation: Truss bridges are built from triangles. At any joint where a diagonal member meets a horizontal beam, the angles form a linear pair (supplementary). Engineers use this relationship to calculate forces and ensure the bridge can bear weight.

image.alt: A truss bridge diagram showing triangular patterns. At a top joint, a diagonal meets the horizontal top chord. The two angles formed are marked as a linear pair (sum to 180°). Force vectors shown.
image.caption: Truss bridges: angles at joints form linear pairs, essential for force calculations.

examples.explanation: The rise and run of a staircase form a right triangle. The angle of inclination and the angle between the stairs and the vertical are complementary (sum to 90°). Building codes specify acceptable angle ranges for safety.

image.alt: Side view of a staircase. The incline angle (35°) and the angle between the stair and vertical (55°) are shown. Equation: 35° + 55° = 90°. Label: 'Stair angle + vertical angle = 90° (complementary)'.
image.caption: Staircase design: the incline angle and the vertical angle are complementary.

categories.description: Compass bearings, course corrections, and relative directions are expressed as angles. Vertical and supplementary relationships help navigators calculate reciprocal courses and avoid obstacles.

examples.explanation: If a ship sails on a course of 45°, the reciprocal (return) course is 45° + 180° = 225°. These two directions differ by 180° — they are supplementary in the sense of forming a straight-line path when reversed.

image.alt: A compass rose showing course 045° (NE) outward and course 225° (SW) return. The 180° difference is marked. Label: 'Outbound + 180° = Return course (supplementary direction concept).'
image.caption: Navigation: reciprocal courses differ by 180°, a supplementary relationship.

examples.explanation: When two lines of sight cross on a map, the angles formed are vertical pairs. Surveyors use the fact that opposite angles are equal to check measurements and calculate distances using triangulation.

image.alt: A map with two intersecting sight lines from survey stations to landmarks. Vertical angles at the intersection are marked equal. Label: 'Vertical angles are equal — used to verify measurements in triangulation.'
image.caption: Surveying: intersecting sight lines create vertical angles used for measurement verification.

categories.description: Visual composition uses angle relationships for balance and aesthetic appeal. Photographers and designers work with angles of view, lighting, and perspective.

examples.process: Rule of thirds and complementary angles
examples.explanation: In photography, the 'rule of thirds' divides the frame into a 3×3 grid. The diagonal from a corner creates complementary angles with the grid lines (summing to 90° at intersections). Photographers use these angles for dynamic composition.

image.alt: A camera viewfinder with rule of thirds grid. A diagonal line from corner creates angles with grid lines. Angles labeled: 37° and 53° at an intersection, summing to 90°. Label: 'Diagonal and grid line form complementary angles.'
image.caption: Photography composition: grid lines and diagonals create complementary angle relationships.

examples.process: Reflections and vertical angles in mirrors
examples.explanation: When light reflects off a mirror, the angle of incidence equals the angle of reflection. These two angles are symmetric about the normal line. The incoming and reflected rays form equal angles with the normal — a relationship analogous to vertical angles or an angle bisector.

image.alt: A mirror with an incoming light ray and reflected ray. Normal line perpendicular to mirror. Angle of incidence (θ) = Angle of reflection (θ). The two angles are marked equal. Label: 'Angle in = Angle out — analogous to congruent angle pairs.'
image.caption: Light reflection: the angle of incidence equals the angle of reflection.

sections.introduction: Mastery of angle pair relationships requires practice identifying pairs in diagrams, applying the correct equation type, and solving for unknown measures. Work through these problems to build accuracy and confidence.

worked_examples.title: Comprehensive Angle Pair Practice
worked_examples.problem:
1) Find the complement of 52° and the supplement of 52°.

2) ∠A and ∠B are complementary. m∠A = 3x, m∠B = x + 10. Find both angles.

3) Two lines intersect. m∠1 = 72°. Find the other three angles.

4) Two lines intersect. m∠1 = 5x − 15, m∠2 = 2x + 30. ∠1 and ∠2 are adjacent. Find x and all four angles.

5) Two lines intersect. m∠3 = 4x + 8, m∠1 = 6x − 12. ∠1 and ∠3 are vertical. Find x and all four angles.

6) An angle is 24° more than its supplement. Find the angle and its supplement.

step_by_step.explanation:
Let the angle = x. Its supplement = 180 − x.

x = (180 − x) + 24

x = 204 − x → 2x = 204 → x = 102°

Supplement = 180° − 102° = 78°. Check: 102° = 78° + 24° ✓

image.alt: Six panels corresponding to the six practice problems: (1) 52° with complement 38° and supplement 128°. (2) Complementary 60° and 30°. (3) Intersection with angles 72°, 108°, 72°, 108°. (4) Linear pair algebra. (5) Vertical pair algebra: 48°, 132°, 48°, 132°. (6) Supplementary word problem: 102° and 78°.
image.caption: Comprehensive angle pair practice: complements, supplements, vertical angles, linear pairs, and word problems.

worked_examples.concept_applied: Complementary (sum to 90°), supplementary/linear pair (sum to 180°), vertical angles (equal). Match equation type to relationship. Solve algebraically.

summary: Angle pairs are classified by their measures and positions. Key points: 1) Adjacent Angles: Share a vertex and a common side, no overlapping interiors. 2) Linear Pair: Adjacent angles whose non-common sides are opposite rays, forming a straight line. By the Linear Pair Postulate, they are always supplementary (sum to 180°). 3) Complementary Angles: Two angles whose measures sum to 90°. Each is the complement of the other. Can be adjacent (forming a right angle) or separate. Use the equation m∠A + m∠B = 90°. 4) Supplementary Angles: Two angles whose measures sum to 180°. Each is the supplement of the other. Linear pairs are always supplementary. Use the equation m∠A + m∠B = 180°. 5) Vertical Angles: Two non-adjacent angles formed by intersecting lines. They are opposite each other. By the Vertical Angles Theorem, vertical angles are always congruent (equal measure). Use the equation m∠1 = m∠3. 6) Solving Strategies: Identify the relationship first, then write the corresponding equation—equality for vertical angles, sum to 90° for complementary, sum to 180° for supplementary/linear pairs. 7) Common errors: Confusing complementary (90°) and supplementary (180°), misidentifying vertical vs. adjacent pairs, using the wrong equation type, and trusting inaccurate diagrams over theorems. Angle pair relationships are essential tools for all geometry involving intersecting lines, parallel lines, triangles, and polygons.

review_questions_image.alt: Concept map with 'Angle Pairs' in center. Branches: Adjacent (share vertex and side), Linear Pair (adjacent + opposite rays, sum 180°), Complementary (sum 90°, adjacent or separate), Supplementary (sum 180°, adjacent or separate), Vertical (opposite at intersection, congruent). Sub-branches: Equation types for each relationship.
review_questions_image.caption: Angle Pairs Review: A visual concept map connecting all angle pair types, their definitions, geometric conditions, and equation forms.
